content.text: What it is

Triage cadence and ownership rules are the operational layer on top of a bug severity and priority matrix. They answer four questions the matrix leaves open: how often does the team formally review the bug queue, who must be present, what decisions must be made by the end of each session, and who owns a bug between sessions when no one is explicitly assigned.

Without these rules, even a well-designed S/P matrix degrades in practice. A P1 bug filed on a Friday afternoon with no on-call DRI and no ownership rule is effectively invisible until Monday morning. Cadence rules close that gap by specifying that a P1 bug must be triaged within two hours of filing, regardless of day or time.
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems produce bugs at a cadence that differs from traditional software. Prompt edge cases surface continuously as users probe the system. Model updates introduce regressions that may not appear until hours after deployment when eval pipelines complete. Tool-call failures cluster around infrastructure events. Without a regular cadence, these bugs accumulate silently in the backlog until a customer escalation forces the team into reactive mode.

Ownership rules matter because AI bugs often do not map cleanly to a single engineer. A hallucination might require a prompt engineer, an ML engineer, and a product manager to diagnose. Default ownership rules — specifying which team owns which bug type before a named engineer is assigned — prevent the diffusion-of-responsibility failure where everyone assumes someone else has the bug.
Benefits
P1 and S1 bugs are guaranteed to be seen within a bounded time window, not just when someone happens to check the backlog.
Every bug leaves triage with a named temporary owner, preventing the 'no one claimed it' failure mode.
Regular cadence creates a forcing function for cleaning stale P3 and P4 bugs before they age into technical debt.
The DRI rotation spreads triage burden across the team rather than concentrating it on a single engineer.
Meeting structure limits triage time to 30 minutes, preventing triage from expanding into a design session.
Default ownership by bug type gives new team members a clear starting point when they receive an unlabeled bug.
Trade-offs
Cadence meetings have overhead. A daily P2 triage meeting costs one meeting slot for every engineer attending. For small teams, this may be disproportionate.
Rotation-based DRI ownership requires every team member to develop enough AI literacy to distinguish a model bug from a prompt bug from an infrastructure bug.
Strict SLA windows for P1 create on-call burden. If P1 triage is required within two hours, someone must be reachable at all times.
Meetings can become rubber-stamp sessions if the DRI has pre-labeled bugs accurately. Consider async-first cadence for P3/P4 bugs to avoid unnecessary synchronous time.
Ownership handoffs between rotation periods can cause bugs in progress to lose context. Require a written summary in the bug before rotating.
How it works

Triage cadence operates at four clock speeds, one per priority tier. P1 and S1 bugs trigger an immediate out-of-band triage: the on-call DRI is notified and must assess the bug within two hours. If the bug is confirmed S1, it immediately escalates to the incident management process. P2 bugs are reviewed in a daily triage slot, typically at the start of the daily standup. P3 bugs are reviewed in a weekly triage meeting. P4 bugs are reviewed during biweekly sprint planning.
DRI rotation:

A designated triage DRI is assigned per week (or per sprint). The DRI is responsible for (a) processing all new bugs filed since the last triage session before the next scheduled meeting, (b) applying or confirming S/P labels, and (c) assigning a temporary owner to every unlabeled or unowned bug. The DRI is not required to fix the bugs — only to ensure the queue is clean.
Ownership rules by bug type:

Hallucination and model regression bugs default to the AI platform team. Prompt and system-prompt configuration bugs default to the product engineering team. Training data and eval correctness bugs default to the data/ML team. Infrastructure bugs (tool call failures, latency, rate limits) default to the infrastructure team. A bug that spans two categories starts with the team whose component caused the failure.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Triage flow diagram: Bug Reported -> DRI Intake -> S/P Assignment (S1? triggers Incident Process) -> Owner Assigned -> Sprint Slot; cadence table showing P1-P4 SLAs and default owners

content.text: The flow has a single mandatory branch: if the bug is classified S1 at the S/P assignment step, it exits the normal triage flow and enters the incident management process immediately. All other bugs continue to owner assignment and then to a sprint slot determined by their priority.
When to use it
Any team operating an AI system in production that receives more than a handful of bug reports per week.
Teams that have experienced surprise P1 bugs discovered by customers before the engineering team.
Teams where AI bugs sit in backlog for weeks because no one knows whose job it is to address them.
Post-launch phases where the volume and nature of bugs shifts from implementation bugs to behavioral and quality bugs.
Teams preparing for an SLA commitment to customers or regulators that requires documented response times by severity.
How to implement
Define the on-call DRI rotation schedule. Use a tool like PagerDuty or OpsGenie to formalize it, including after-hours coverage for P1 triage.
Set up an alert: any bug filed with S1 or P1 must immediately page the on-call DRI. Do not rely on email for P1 notifications.
Create a recurring calendar event for P2 daily triage (15 minutes, at the start of standup) and P3 weekly triage (30 minutes, fixed day and time).
Write the default ownership matrix in the team wiki. Map each bug type to the team that owns it by default. Paste a link to this matrix in every new bug template.

Define the triage meeting agenda in writing: (a) review all bugs without an S/P label, (b) review all bugs without an owner, (c) confirm or adjust S/P labels on escalated bugs. No agenda item should take more than three minutes per bug.
At the end of each triage rotation, require the outgoing DRI to write a brief handoff note: how many bugs were triaged, any recurring patterns, any bugs awaiting information from reporters.
Examples

# DRI rotation schedule — one week per person
triage_rotation:
  cadence: weekly            # rotate each Monday 09:00
  on_call_for_p1: true       # paged for P1 outside business hours
  members:
    - name: alice
      week_start: "2026-07-20"
    - name: bob
      week_start: "2026-07-27"
    - name: carol
      week_start: "2026-08-03"
  handoff:
    required: true
    template: |
      ## Triage handoff — week of {{ week_start }}
      Bugs processed: {{ count }}
      Open P1/P2 at handoff: {{ open_p1_p2 }}
      Recurring patterns: {{ patterns }}
      Awaiting reporter response: {{ awaiting }}

## Triage checklist (per-bug, at intake)

- [ ] S/P label assigned (S1–S4 / P1–P4)
- [ ] Bug type identified: hallucination | regression | configuration | infrastructure | eval
- [ ] Temporary owner assigned (default by type if no volunteer)
- [ ] SLA deadline set (computed from priority)
- [ ] If S1: incident process triggered immediately
- [ ] Reproducible? If no, note reproducibility rate estimate
- [ ] Affected user % estimated
- [ ] Safety-adjacent? If yes, S/P modifier applied
Real-world example

A customer success manager files a bug at 6:45 PM on a Tuesday: the AI assistant is generating responses that occasionally include the names of other customers' companies in the reply, suggesting the context window is bleeding across sessions. The PagerDuty integration recognizes the keyword 'cross-session' as a potential privacy flag and pages the on-call DRI within minutes.

The DRI reviews the bug at 7:02 PM. The issue matches S1 criteria (PII-adjacent: customer names from other sessions appearing in output). The DRI applies S1/P1 and triggers the incident process. The infrastructure team (default owner for session-isolation bugs) is paged. By 9:30 PM the root cause — a session caching misconfiguration in the context assembly layer — is identified and a fix is deployed. The triage cadence rule that required a two-hour maximum response for P1 bugs meant the issue was caught and resolved the same evening rather than sitting until the next morning standup.
Common mistakes
Treating the weekly triage meeting as the only triage venue. P1 bugs cannot wait a week; they require immediate out-of-band response.

Assigning DRI duties without training. The DRI must be able to read model evaluation scores, tool call traces, and LLM cost dashboards. A DRI who cannot distinguish a model regression from a prompt misconfiguration will mis-label bugs.
Rotating DRI too frequently (daily). Daily rotation creates context-switching overhead and prevents the DRI from seeing patterns across bugs filed during the week.
Not requiring a handoff note. Without a written summary, incoming DRIs start cold with no knowledge of ongoing issues.
Allowing triage meetings to expand into root-cause analysis. Triage assigns S/P and an owner; diagnosis happens in a separate session with the assigned owner.
Skipping the agenda for P4 bugs at biweekly sprint planning. P4 bugs that never get reviewed accumulate until the backlog is too large to manage.
Best practices
Run the P2 daily triage as an async-first process: the DRI reviews new bugs before standup and arrives with labels filled in. Standup is for exceptions and questions, not for labeling from scratch.
Keep a 'triage backlog health' metric: the count of bugs in the queue that have been open for longer than two times their priority SLA. Review this metric weekly and set a team target of zero.

Use a Slack channel (or equivalent) as the triage notification hub. Every new bug creates a message in the channel; DRI confirms label and owner by reacting to the message. This creates a lightweight audit trail without requiring a synchronous meeting for every bug.
For agentic AI, require the DRI to attach the tool call trace or eval run ID to every infrastructure and regression bug before assigning it. A bug without its trace is much harder to diagnose.
Establish an escalation SLA: if the assigned owner has not acknowledged the bug within one SLA period after assignment, the DRI re-pings and escalates to the engineering manager.
Review the ownership matrix at least once per quarter. As the system evolves, the boundary between product-eng and AI-platform may shift.
Performance and scalability

At low bug volume (fewer than 20 bugs per week), a weekly triage meeting is sufficient for P3/P4 bugs and the DRI can handle P1/P2 async. At medium volume (20–100 bugs per week), the daily P2 triage meeting becomes necessary to prevent queue buildup. Above 100 bugs per week, consider splitting the triage DRI role: one DRI for model/prompt bugs, another for infrastructure bugs.

Automated pre-labeling (a heuristic classifier at intake) is the most effective scaling lever. If 70% of bugs are correctly pre-labeled by the classifier, the DRI reviews and confirms rather than starting from scratch. This reduces per-bug DRI time from several minutes to under 30 seconds for clear-cut cases.
Tooling and ecosystem
PagerDuty and OpsGenie provide rotation scheduling, escalation chains, and acknowledgment tracking for P1 triage SLAs.
Linear and Jira both support SLA deadline fields and automated reminders when a deadline approaches.
Slack workflows can be used to create a triage bot: a new bug in the tracker creates a Slack message; the DRI assigns S/P and owner via emoji reaction or slash command.
GitHub Actions or similar CI systems can open bug tickets automatically when an eval suite regression exceeds a threshold, with the eval run ID attached.
Notion, Confluence, or a plain git-tracked markdown file can host the ownership matrix and triage checklists so they are versioned alongside the codebase.
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
P1 response time
Ownership clarity
Key weakness
Ad hoc triage
Hours to days
None
P1 bugs missed until escalation
Sprint-only review
Up to two weeks
Assigned at sprint planning
P1 issues cannot wait a sprint
Dedicated triage team
Minutes
High
Expensive; creates silo
Rotation-based DRI (this doc)
Two hours for P1, daily for P2
High via default ownership rules
Requires AI literacy in all DRI candidates
Fully automated triage

Security and reliability

S1 bugs that involve potential data exposure must be handled under a confidential channel. Do not post the bug details in a public Slack channel; use a private incident channel with only the people needed to resolve it. Some organizations require that S1 security bugs be logged in a separate, access-controlled tracker rather than the general bug tracker.

Reliability of the cadence itself is a process concern: if the on-call DRI is unavailable and there is no backup, the P1 triage SLA fails. PagerDuty escalation chains should have at least two levels: primary DRI, then engineering manager. Audit the escalation chain quarterly to ensure contact information is current.
Mental model

Think of the triage cadence as a set of conveyor belts running at different speeds. P1 bugs land on a belt moving at 2 hours per cycle. P2 bugs land on a belt moving at 24 hours per cycle. P3 bugs move at 7 days per cycle; P4 at 14 days. Any bug that misses its belt — an unlabeled bug with no owner — falls off and gets lost. The DRI is the person standing at the intake end of all four belts, making sure every bug lands on the right one.

Ownership rules are the routing labels on each bug. Without them, bugs reach the end of the belt and fall to the floor because no one knows which team is supposed to pick them up. Default ownership by bug type is the fallback label: even without a named engineer, the bug knows which team it belongs to.
Key takeaways
Triage cadence and ownership rules are the operational contract that makes a bug severity matrix work in practice.
P1 bugs must be triaged within two hours of filing, not at the next scheduled meeting.
Every bug must leave triage with a named owner; default ownership by bug type provides the fallback when no engineer volunteers.
The DRI rotation distributes triage burden and requires every DRI to maintain enough AI literacy to classify model, prompt, infrastructure, and eval bugs.
A 30-minute weekly triage meeting is the right venue for P3 bugs; P2 bugs belong in a daily async review; P4 bugs belong in sprint planning.
When a bug is classified S1 in triage, it immediately exits the triage process and enters incident management.
Measure triage backlog health (bugs open beyond 2x their SLA) as a leading indicator of process breakdown.
